speaker: interviewer
text: It is December 21, 1994. What were you saying about, you did not like the gefilte fish in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too dark and too sweet. And they had no horseradish and I couldn't-- [LAUGHS] I couldn't eat it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is on the account of the Polish people. You know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, even now when I make gefilte fish, I put a little sugar, just to please him. But very little. I-- I can't-- you see, Sephardim don't like sweet with-- with the cooking. We like lemon and parsley and you know, piquant, you know, things that are more-- so that was not exactly my taste. But I learned a little bit too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you something. It's a little intimate. But the fact that Mr. Shear never had parents, he was-- he never--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was brought up in a family. How-- how does it affect his own surrounding with a family, with children, with grandchildren? How does he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it doesn't affect-- I think he-- he really-- I have a little bit of family here. And he-- he took to my family very well, and everything. Well, he had no other choice. He had no family so he had to-- to adapt himself to my family. You know? And-- and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't so bad to be married.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had never-- I always wanted to be a part of a family, whether or not being if you're related. You know, [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when he goes to Israel, he goes and visits. You know, he has a niece and a nephew. You know. Because from his half sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: A brother, if you are not close with it, is-- is you are better off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister, how old is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she has a daughter. The daughter is 53. She was here visiting with us a couple of weeks ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple months ago. And she has a son who is two years older, David is. So they live in Israel, they're quite well, quite. They have nice families and they have nice homes. And they established themselves pretty well. And when I go there, I spend as much time as possible. But mostly I spend time with some friends which I accumulated in Israel. And some nice friends. And as I said, I love Israel. And whenever I go-- I'm at home. People, of course, compliment me on my Hebrew. Which it's not so good. It's just good, what things that I know. That is, I guess, my-- my Hebrew is-- is-- [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]. Well, to be able-- to-- to be in the market and to sell yourself, you've got to know the market language. You know? It is the slang and all. That's what makes today's modern Hebrew. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you regret that you didn't make [NON-ENGLISH] to Israel as a youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Heavens no. I regret that I could have been a part, maybe. Maybe if I would've started in Israel, with my knowledge and dedication to social work, maybe I would've been even a part of some social programs in Israel. I might've even-- maybe-- maybe would have been somewhere else in the headlines. But looking at my life here, my family, my wife, and daughter and the children, I-- I'm very proud. And I'm very-- I look back and I think, everything is just made up for a certain of-- certain, not for everything, but almost for everything that I missed during my youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your education was mostly in Yeshiva. So Yeshiva--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- it was in between that while I was in the-- my going to Yeshiva, I also was a part of the Jewish youth organizations. Which I went to the Tarbut, to [NON-ENGLISH], to-- to-- they-- you know, as you go on-- I mean, I-- they-- they knew that I needed a general education at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew that I'm a Yeshiva not by choice. I got in because I had no other way. But it was a part of the curriculum that I was sent to be able to-- first of all, I-- I was writing all the time. So I-- somehow, as they say, practice makes perfect. So-- Do you keep Shabbat and the holidays here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we have a-- we have a Kosher home. We're-- we have. But we're mostly traditional. In other words, we-- we observe the holidays and we observe the Kashrut. But we're not completely, you know, to the extremes, you know, not to go out on Saturday--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to go, you know-- we-- we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's put it this way. I feel guilty if I don't-- if I put on the-- the light on Shabbat to make coffee. I do it, but I feel guilty about it. You know, it's just-- or the other things that I do. I might compromise. But I know about it. I'm aware of it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say, I don't enjoy it to the fullest extent when I eat outside. And sometimes I eat with non-- it's not-- Not kosher, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Kosher, I feel guilty about it. But as they say, it is a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They say one commandment that God gave that's not written in the 613 commandments. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He said to Adam, before he even gave any religion, he said, [SPEAKING HEBREW] He said, see, I have created everything for your benefit. Please, don't spoil it. Don't destroy it. [? Which, ?] you are trying to make so many improvements, you'll destroy it. And that's what we are doing today, with the ozone and all these things. We are trying to improve our system of growing things and in productions. And we are destroying our environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with me, it's-- I'm trying to stay in the-- in the frame of going on and surviving. And the rest is-- that's between me and the Almighty. The rest is history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just a sec. Mr. Shear, can you describe this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the one-- the first one is a-- if you can see at the bottom, it's the military government of Germany, 1945. it is my first document. That I am who I am. The name, as you can see, David Sukaczer, and born. And they verified that I just-- --was liberated from concentration camp in Langenstein. And today, that I legitimately am looking for friends or family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one is also from the city of Goslaw, which the Organization of Displaced People, which they verify that I am on my way of-- of finding some relatives. Because at the time, they thought I'm gonna go back to Poland. And they ask anybody for his assistance, to help me on my way of my journey. And I think there is one more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. No, he can leave the finger on in this case.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] And this is also from the-- where I was liberated. And this was on the Russian side, which was Russia later. But also from this, the Volksfront, a different organization. Everyone tried to show that they are-- that they are authority. So they also verify that I-- that my concentration number is B-5278, and the-- and-- and in Buchenwald was 120883. And they also-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first-- the first shot that you see, right after the war, this was in Wernigerode, as they see. This was on the East side of East Germany. Sort of I'm still wearing my cap, from which I kept as a souvenir, which I was wearing in the-- in Auschwitz. This is the number one. The second one is me being in the-- in-- in Goslaw, on this committee, which we took care of a lot of young people. And this is a part of the children, which they were all already born. It was probably, you know, after a while. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And this is a part of our group which we were sort of on an outing of planning the-- how to continue to-- to organize things. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] I am here on the left, sort of. Yeah, this is me. And here, of course, you can see this is me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of most of the-- the friends. We were a gathering of all the survivors. As you can see, everyone is trying to squeeze together. This is after we sort of stopped on an-- on an outing, probably, somewheres.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sitting down below, on the-- and holding my-- adjusting my beret. From the tree, I am the one on the left. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is, as you can see on top it's written, Goldene Krone. This is the hotel that we took over. And we made it sort of as a camp while-- or as a hotel for all the refugees who came to the city, this was the place which they came and they got free lodging and food. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the most important picture of them all, is myself getting out from the American Embassy after-- after finding out that I am-- I made the grade to-- to-- to go to United States. As you can see, the smile on my face that I was very happy to hear the news, the good news. You see the emblem of the United States Embassy. OK, that's about it. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And this, of course, is my pride and joy, what I worked all my life for, thank God. This is my daughter and her husband. And-- and this is a-- a wedding picture. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is-- her name is Gabriel Flory. And his name is David Parrish. They're Mr. And Mrs. Parrish. They live in Minneapolis. And thank God, they have a wonderful family. Which I'm going to show the-- the children. You want to give me the-- OK, to complete this picture, is-- this is the rest of my family. Here, again, is my daughter and son-in-law. And my great joy by the-- the two boys, Joshua and [? Rubin. ?] We call him [? Rubu. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Joshua is with your-- your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joshua is with my daughter. And [? Rubu, ?] as he is called, is with his dad. And thank God, and God bless them.
